RISC-V Integrated Data-stream Offloading Accelerator Proposal of Work

Guo Ren
  • All Messages By This Member
Mar 19   #216  
Edited Mar 19

RISC-V Integrated Data-stream Offloading Accelerator Proposal of Work

Proposer

Name: GUO Ren & CHEN Jiankang
Organization/Affiliation: Alibaba DAMO & Cloud

Introduction

The RISC-V Integrated Data-stream Offloading Accelerator (RVI-DOA) is a proposed integrated hardware accelerator for RISC-V platforms. It enables efficient offloading of data-processing operations directly from the CPU to improve throughput and reduce CPU utilization & Cache occupition compared to single-threaded CPU copying, whether in kernel or user space, with minimal synchronization overhead. These data-processing operations include:
  • memcpy / memmove
  • memcmp
  • memset
  • CRC computation
  • compression / decompression
  • PTW dirty bit clearing (Co-work with CPU/IOMMU Dirty Page Logging Processing Accelerator)
This acceleration is especially beneficial for performance-critical OS workloads, such as:
  • Linux page migration (NUMA balancing, compaction, memory hotplug, CXL tiering, DAMON)
  • VM Live migration
  • Linux zram compressed swapping, EROFS segment decompression
  • Memory deduplication (KSM)
  • Other bulk memory manipulation tasks
This proposal aims to deliver RISC-V-native, on-die data-streaming acceleration capabilities comparable to Intel DSA, AMD PTDMA/AE4DMA, or IBM zEDC, but tailored to the open RISC-V ISA ecosystem.

Why Existing Extensions Are Insufficient?

There is no platform specification for data-stream offloading accelerator (e.g., Intel DSA, AMD PTDMA/AE4DMA, and IBM zEDC) around all RISC-V specifications.

Definitions

  • RVI-DOA - RISC-V Integrated Data-stream Offloading Accelerator
  • Intel DSA - Intel Data Streaming Accelerator
  • AMD PTDMA - AMD Pass-Through DMA Engine
  • AMD AE4DMA - AMD Engine 4 DMA
  • IBM zEDC - Integrated Accelerator for zEnterprise Data Compression
  • CRC - Cyclic Redundancy Check
  • EROFS - Enhanced Read-Only File System in the Linux kernel
  • KSM - Kernel Same-page Merging
  • PTW - Page Table Walk
  • DSWQ - Device Shared Work Queue-based programming interface
  • DWQ - Dedicated Work Queue-based programming interface

Background

Modern operating systems face growing pressure from memory-intensive workloads in data centers, cloud computing, virtualization, and edge devices. Operations such as bulk memory movement, comparisons, initialization, checksum computation, and compression/decompression consume most CPU cycles on the critical OS path. These include NUMA balancing, memory compaction, page migration during memory hotplug or CXL tiering, VM live migration, zram compressed swapping, EROFS segment decompression, and Kernel Same-page Merging (KSM). Traditional software implementations (e.g., memcpy, memset in mm/migrate.c or crypto/zlib) become severe bottlenecks on large folios (2 MB+) and high-bandwidth memory systems, consuming excessive CPU resources (Private-cache & Cycles) and limiting overall system throughput.
To address this, leading proprietary architectures have introduced integrated, on-die data-streaming accelerators that offload these operations directly from the CPU with minimal synchronization overhead:
  • Intel Data Streaming Accelerator (DSA), introduced in 4th-Gen Xeon Scalable processors (Sapphire Rapids, 2023) and enhanced in subsequent generations (DSA 3.0 support landed in Linux 7.0, February 2026), provides high-performance hardware support for memcpy/memmove, memcmp, memset, CRC, and batch transformations. The idxd driver enables seamless integration between the kernel and user space. Research published in 2025 demonstrates concrete gains: the IEEE paper ÔÇťAccelerating Page Migrations in Operating Systems With Intel DSAÔÇŁ shows up to 1.2├Ś faster migrate_pages() throughput of kcompactd; the DSA-2LM tiered-memory system (USENIX ATC 2025) delivers 16ÔÇô30 % application-level performance improvement over state-of-the-art tiering solutions by offloading page migration and reducing CPU overhead.
  • AMD PassThru DMA (PTDMA) (EPYC Rome/Milan) and its successor, AE4DMA (Genoa/Siena+, driver merged into Linux 6.14 in 2025), are specialized high-bandwidth memory-to-memory DMA engines. The 2026 LWN-reported RFC v4, ÔÇťAccelerate page migration with batch copying and hardware offloadÔÇŁ (Shivank Garg, AMD), introduces the migrate_offload framework with the reference driver dcbm. On Zen 3 EPYC, this yields ~6├Ś throughput improvement for 2 MB folios (66.86 GB/s vs. 10.88 GB/s baseline) and dramatically lowers CPU utilization, even with a single DMA channel, outperforming software for batches Ôëą8 pages.
  • IBM zEnterprise Data Compression (zEDC), integrated on-chip since z15 (2019), uses the DFLTCC instruction for hardware-accelerated deflate-compatible compression/decompression. Kernel support added in 2020 (lib/zlib_deflate) benefits zram, btrfs, and other compressed paths on IBM Z mainframes.
These accelerators prove that hardware offload can deliver multi-fold speedups, free CPU cores for useful work, and enable new system-level optimizations (e.g., CPU-free tiered memory, faster live migration, higher-density containers).
However, the RISC-V ecosystem currently lacks any ratified platform specification for a comparable integrated data-stream offloading accelerator. While the Atomic I/O Enqueue (AIOE) extension provides low-overhead work submission primitives, it does not define the accelerator hardware interface, supported operations, or standardized Linux integration. Existing RISC-V vector or custom accelerator extensions remain either too general or vendor-specific, preventing portable, cross-vendor adoption in Linux, KVM, or user-space libraries.
The absence of a unified RISC-V-native solution creates a clear performance and ecosystem gap compared to x86 and IBM platforms. Establishing the RISC-V Integrated Data-stream Offloading Accelerator (RVI-DOA) specification addresses this gap by providing an open, RISC-V Oriented, on-die accelerator model that can be implemented by any RISC-V vendor. It directly enables the same high-impact use cases already proven on other architectures, while improving RISC-V ISA extensions (e.g., co-working with future Dirty Page Logging via accelerating PTW dirty-bit clearing). This proposal, therefore, aims to make RISC-V provide competitive data-stream processing performance across data-center, cloud, virtualization, and embedded scenarios.

Objectives

The RVI-DOA specification defines a standardized, open, and vendor-agnostic data-stream accelerator interface for RISC-V platforms. The objectives are:
  • Survey mainstream accelerator solutions in the market. Conduct a comprehensive study of existing high-performance data-streaming accelerators, including Intel DSA, AMD PTDMA/AE4DMA, and IBM zEDC, to understand their architectures, programming models, performance characteristics, and integration approaches.
  1. Analyze real-world OS and virtualization usage scenarios. Identify and analyze the most CPU-intensive memory operations in modern operating systems, including NUMA balancing, memory compaction, page migration during memory hotplug or CXL tiering, VM live migration, zram compressed swapping, EROFS segment decompression, Data Access Monitoring (DAMON), and Kernel Same-page Merging (KSM).
  2. Define a heterogeneous programming interface. Specify a flexible and efficient programming model based on DWQ (Dedicated Work Queue) or DSWQ (Device Shared Work Queue) to support both single-threaded high-performance and multi-threaded/high-concurrency submission scenarios with minimal synchronization overhead.
  3. Define the supported functionality. Clearly define the core operations to be accelerated, including bulk memory movement (memcpy/memmove), comparisons (memcmp), initialization (memset), bitwise operations (memand/or/xor), checksum computation (CRC), and compression/decompression, PTW dirty bit clearing, along with any additional features required for kernel and user-space integration.
  4. Comprehensive virtualization support. Provide full guest-direct offload capability (no hypervisor trapping for accelerator submission), seamless integration with RISC-V ISA extensions.
These objectives ensure that RVI-DOA is built on a solid understanding of both existing hardware solutions and actual OS workloads, while delivering a clean, portable, and extensible specification for the RISC-V ecosystem.

Exclusions

To keep the scope narrow and enable fast ratification, the RVI-DOA specification excludes the following:
  • Any cryptographic algorithms (AES, SHA, SM3/SM4, etc.)
  • General-purpose compute, vector, tensor, or AI/ML operations.
  • Networking packet processing and storage-specific features (RAID, erasure coding)
This specification focuses exclusively on memory-movement data-stream processing (memcpy/memmove, memcmp, memset, CRC, and basic compression/decompression) for OS workloads.

Collaborations

  • Datacenter SIG
  • Hypervisors SIG
  • Tech-iommu TG
  • SOC Infrastructure (HC)
  • ISA Infrastructure (HC)
  • Privileged Software (HC)

Governing Committee

SoC Infrastructure HC (HC)

Sponsoring Organizations

These Premier and Strategic Members support this Proposal:
  1. Alibaba (Premier) - GUO Ren & CHEN Jiankang
 
 
Greg Favor
  • All Messages By This Member
Mar 19   #217  

It sounds like this is not an ISA extension (which is also reflected in not being sent to either ISA email list) - although the text claims that the Priv ISA would be the governing committee!?

As a non-ISA extension there is the matter of whether this should be standardized by RVI.  AMD only supported memory copy/DMA operations.   And ARM, I don't believe, has standardized anything like this, nor do any ARM-based servers shipping in volume provide this.  And note that the companies that _have_ standardized something like this, did so as part of SoC products.

In any case, there is value to this type of functionality.  But ARM hasn't architected anything like this.  And only SoC vendors have provided varying degrees of this.  So the question is whether this is something that RVI would standardize versus viewing this as in the realm of SoC vendors providing system-level value-add.

Greg

toggle quoted message Show quoted text

From: sig-datacenter@... <sig-datacenter@...> on behalf of Guo Ren via lists.riscv.org <guoren1983=gmail.com@...>
Sent: Wednesday, March 18, 2026 8:23 PM
To: ŔľŤňŞů <shawn.xs@...>; ŠľçńżĹ <songzhuo.sz@...>; Úâşń╗╗ <guoren.gr@...>; sig-datacenter <sig-datacenter@...>; sig-hypervisors@... <sig-hypervisors@...>; tech-iommu@... <tech-iommu@...>; soc-infra@... <soc-infra@...>; yubin.cjk@... <yubin.cjk@...>
Subject: [RISC-V] [sig-datacenter] RISC-V Integrated Data-stream Offloading Accelerator Proposal of Work
 

WARNING: This email originated from outside of Qualcomm. Please be wary of any links or attachments, and do not enable macros.

RISC-V Integrated Data-stream Offloading Accelerator Proposal of Work

Proposer

Name: GUO Ren & CHEN Jiankang
Organization/Affiliation: Alibaba DAMO & Cloud

Introduction

The RISC-V Integrated Data-stream Offloading Accelerator (RVI-DOA) is a proposed integrated hardware accelerator for RISC-V platforms. It enables efficient offloading of data-processing operations directly from the CPU to improve throughput and reduce CPU utilization & Cache occupition compared to single-threaded CPU copying, whether in kernel or user space, with minimal synchronization overhead. These data-processing operations include:
  • memcpy / memmove
  • memcmp
  • memset
  • CRC computation
  • compression / decompression
  • PTW dirty bit clearing (Co-work with CPU/IOMMU Dirty Page Logging Processing Accelerator)
This acceleration is especially beneficial for performance-critical OS workloads, such as:
  • Linux page migration (NUMA balancing, compaction, memory hotplug, CXL tiering, DAMON)
  • VM Live migration
  • Linux zram compressed swapping, EROFS segment decompression
  • Memory deduplication (KSM)
  • Other bulk memory manipulation tasks
This proposal aims to deliver RISC-V-native, on-die data-streaming acceleration capabilities comparable to Intel DSA, AMD PTDMA/AE4DMA, or IBM zEDC, but tailored to the open RISC-V ISA ecosystem.

Why Existing Extensions Are Insufficient?

There is no platform specification for data-stream offloading accelerator (e.g., Intel DSA, AMD PTDMA/AE4DMA, and IBM zEDC) around all RISC-V specifications.

Definitions

  • RVI-DOA - RISC-V Integrated Data-stream Offloading Accelerator
  • Intel DSA - Intel Data Streaming Accelerator
  • AMD PTDMA - AMD Pass-Through DMA Engine
  • AMD AE4DMA - AMD Engine 4 DMA
  • IBM zEDC - Integrated Accelerator for zEnterprise Data Compression
  • CRC - Cyclic Redundancy Check
  • EROFS - Enhanced Read-Only File System in the Linux kernel
  • KSM - Kernel Same-page Merging
  • PTW - Page Table Walk
  • DSWQ - Device Shared Work Queue-based programming interface
  • DWQ - Dedicated Work Queue-based programming interface

Background

Modern operating systems face growing pressure from memory-intensive workloads in data centers, cloud computing, virtualization, and edge devices. Operations such as bulk memory movement, comparisons, initialization, checksum computation, and compression/decompression consume most CPU cycles on the critical OS path. These include NUMA balancing, memory compaction, page migration during memory hotplug or CXL tiering, VM live migration, zram compressed swapping, EROFS segment decompression, and Kernel Same-page Merging (KSM). Traditional software implementations (e.g., memcpy, memset in mm/migrate.c or crypto/zlib) become severe bottlenecks on large folios (2 MB+) and high-bandwidth memory systems, consuming excessive CPU resources (Private-cache & Cycles) and limiting overall system throughput.
To address this, leading proprietary architectures have introduced integrated, on-die data-streaming accelerators that offload these operations directly from the CPU with minimal synchronization overhead:
  • Intel Data Streaming Accelerator (DSA), introduced in 4th-Gen Xeon Scalable processors (Sapphire Rapids, 2023) and enhanced in subsequent generations (DSA 3.0 support landed in Linux 7.0, February 2026), provides high-performance hardware support for memcpy/memmove, memcmp, memset, CRC, and batch transformations. The idxd driver enables seamless integration between the kernel and user space. Research published in 2025 demonstrates concrete gains: the IEEE paper ÔÇťAccelerating Page Migrations in Operating Systems With Intel DSAÔÇŁ shows up to 1.2├Ś faster migrate_pages() throughput of kcompactd; the DSA-2LM tiered-memory system (USENIX ATC 2025) delivers 16ÔÇô30 % application-level performance improvement over state-of-the-art tiering solutions by offloading page migration and reducing CPU overhead.
  • AMD PassThru DMA (PTDMA) (EPYC Rome/Milan) and its successor, AE4DMA (Genoa/Siena+, driver merged into Linux 6.14 in 2025), are specialized high-bandwidth memory-to-memory DMA engines. The 2026 LWN-reported RFC v4, ÔÇťAccelerate page migration with batch copying and hardware offloadÔÇŁ (Shivank Garg, AMD), introduces the migrate_offload framework with the reference driver dcbm. On Zen 3 EPYC, this yields ~6├Ś throughput improvement for 2 MB folios (66.86 GB/s vs. 10.88 GB/s baseline) and dramatically lowers CPU utilization, even with a single DMA channel, outperforming software for batches Ôëą8 pages.
  • IBM zEnterprise Data Compression (zEDC), integrated on-chip since z15 (2019), uses the DFLTCC instruction for hardware-accelerated deflate-compatible compression/decompression. Kernel support added in 2020 (lib/zlib_deflate) benefits zram, btrfs, and other compressed paths on IBM Z mainframes.
These accelerators prove that hardware offload can deliver multi-fold speedups, free CPU cores for useful work, and enable new system-level optimizations (e.g., CPU-free tiered memory, faster live migration, higher-density containers).
However, the RISC-V ecosystem currently lacks any ratified platform specification for a comparable integrated data-stream offloading accelerator. While the Atomic I/O Enqueue (AIOE) extension provides low-overhead work submission primitives, it does not define the accelerator hardware interface, supported operations, or standardized Linux integration. Existing RISC-V vector or custom accelerator extensions remain either too general or vendor-specific, preventing portable, cross-vendor adoption in Linux, KVM, or user-space libraries.
The absence of a unified RISC-V-native solution creates a clear performance and ecosystem gap compared to x86 and IBM platforms. Establishing the RISC-V Integrated Data-stream Offloading Accelerator (RVI-DOA) specification addresses this gap by providing an open, RISC-V Oriented, on-die accelerator model that can be implemented by any RISC-V vendor. It directly enables the same high-impact use cases already proven on other architectures, while improving RISC-V ISA extensions (e.g., co-working with future Dirty Page Logging via accelerating PTW dirty-bit clearing). This proposal, therefore, aims to make RISC-V provide competitive data-stream processing performance across data-center, cloud, virtualization, and embedded scenarios.

Objectives

The RVI-DOA specification defines a standardized, open, and vendor-agnostic data-stream accelerator interface for RISC-V platforms. The objectives are:
  • Survey mainstream accelerator solutions in the market. Conduct a comprehensive study of existing high-performance data-streaming accelerators, including Intel DSA, AMD PTDMA/AE4DMA, and IBM zEDC, to understand their architectures, programming models, performance characteristics, and integration approaches.
  1. Analyze real-world OS and virtualization usage scenarios. Identify and analyze the most CPU-intensive memory operations in modern operating systems, including NUMA balancing, memory compaction, page migration during memory hotplug or CXL tiering, VM live migration, zram compressed swapping, EROFS segment decompression, Data Access Monitoring (DAMON), and Kernel Same-page Merging (KSM).
  2. Define a heterogeneous programming interface. Specify a flexible and efficient programming model based on DWQ (Dedicated Work Queue) or DSWQ (Device Shared Work Queue) to support both single-threaded high-performance and multi-threaded/high-concurrency submission scenarios with minimal synchronization overhead.
  3. Define the supported functionality. Clearly define the core operations to be accelerated, including bulk memory movement (memcpy/memmove), comparisons (memcmp), initialization (memset), bitwise operations (memand/or/xor), checksum computation (CRC), and compression/decompression, PTW dirty bit clearing, along with any additional features required for kernel and user-space integration.
  4. Comprehensive virtualization support. Provide full guest-direct offload capability (no hypervisor trapping for accelerator submission), seamless integration with RISC-V ISA extensions.
These objectives ensure that RVI-DOA is built on a solid understanding of both existing hardware solutions and actual OS workloads, while delivering a clean, portable, and extensible specification for the RISC-V ecosystem.

Exclusions

To keep the scope narrow and enable fast ratification, the RVI-DOA specification excludes the following:
  • Any cryptographic algorithms (AES, SHA, SM3/SM4, etc.)
  • General-purpose compute, vector, tensor, or AI/ML operations.
  • Networking packet processing and storage-specific features (RAID, erasure coding)
This specification focuses exclusively on memory-movement data-stream processing (memcpy/memmove, memcmp, memset, CRC, and basic compression/decompression) for OS workloads.
